title: The brachistochrone: the curve that beat the straight line
intro: In the June 1696 issue of Acta Eruditorum, the Leipzig mathematical journal Leibniz had founded fourteen years earlier, Johann Bernoulli published an open challenge to 'the most acute mathematicians flourishing throughout the world.' Given two points A and B in a vertical plane, with B lower than A but not directly beneath it, find the curve along which a bead sliding from rest under gravity alone gets from A to B in the least possible time. Five solutions came back over the next nine months, one of them famously postmarked from London the morning after the problem reached Newton, and the curve every author found was the same: the cycloid, the path traced by a fixed point on the rim of a rolling wheel. The brachistochrone became the founding puzzle of the calculus of variations, the apparatus that later gave physics its principle of least action.

blocks.text: Johann Bernoulli laid the problem out cleanly in the June 1696 Acta Eruditorum. Given two points A and B in a vertical plane, B below A but not directly under it, find the curve down which a bead sliding from rest, with no friction, gets from A to B in the least time. He called the curve the brachistochrone, from the Greek brachistos for shortest and chronos for time. He gave the world six months. The deadline was a small act of teasing: Bernoulli already had the answer and wanted to see who else could find it.

Six months passed and only one solution had arrived, from Leibniz, who had asked Bernoulli to extend the deadline because nobody else seemed to be working. The new deadline was Easter 1697. Five solutions came in. Johann Bernoulli published his own; his brother James (Jacob) sent a competing solution from Basel; Leibniz sent another; the Marquis de l'Hôpital sent a fourth (probably with help from Johann himself, his on-and-off tutor); and an anonymous solution arrived from London. Johann opened the London packet, glanced at the geometric construction inside, and reportedly said 'tanquam ex ungue leonem,' I know the lion by his claw.

Newton was fifty-four, had not done serious mathematics in eighteen months, and was spending his days running the Royal Mint, where he was personally interrogating clipping forgers and supervising the great recoinage of English silver. The diary of his niece Catherine Conduitt records that he came home from the Mint at four in the afternoon with the problem in hand, sat down at the table, and stayed there until he had finished it. He sent the result back the next morning, signed only with his initials. The Bernoullis already had their answers; the brachistochrone contest was settled less by difficulty than by who was working on what, but it became, like Newton's anonymous calculation, a public proof that the engine still ran.

sections.heading: 2. Why the straight line is not the answer

blocks.text: Set the problem up the way Bernoulli set it up. Place A at the origin and B at coordinates (L, H) with H positive, measured as depth below A. The horizontal extent of the candidate curves is L; the vertical drop they all share is H. A bead at depth y has, by energy conservation, kinetic energy m v² / 2 equal to potential energy lost m g y, so its speed is v(y) = √(2gy) at that depth no matter which curve it has been sliding along. The total descent time along any candidate curve is the integral of arc length ds divided by speed v at each point:

blocks.caption: T is the total descent time. ds is the arc-length element along the candidate curve, the distance the bead actually travels in an infinitesimal step. v is the bead's speed at that point. y is the depth below the starting height A. g is the acceleration of gravity. The integral runs along the curve from A to B, and the speed at each point is fixed by how far the bead has dropped, not by the path it took to get there.

blocks.text: Carry out the integral for the obvious candidate first, the straight line from (0, 0) to (L, H). Parametrize by horizontal position x running from 0 to L. The curve is y = (H/L) x, the slope is dy/dx = H/L, the arc-length element is ds = √(1 + H²/L²) dx, and the speed at horizontal position x is v = √(2 g (H/L) x). Substituting and pulling the constants out of the integral gives a closed form:

blocks.caption: T_straight is the descent time along the straight line from A to B. L is the horizontal extent of the descent. H is the vertical drop. g is gravity. The (L² + H²) under the square root is the square of the straight-line length from A to B. The H in the denominator is the part that says a steeper drop ends the bead's race sooner: doubling H at fixed L cuts T_straight by roughly √2.

blocks.text: Nothing in the form of the time integral says the straight line is special. Consider instead a path that drops nearly vertically for the first instant and then runs nearly horizontal across to B. The bead picks up its full final speed √(2gH) in the first short stretch, and then traverses almost the entire remaining length of path at that maximum speed. The horizontal-running part has more arc length than the straight line had, but it is covered at high v. The straight line, by contrast, spends as much time near the slow top as near the fast bottom. Trading length for speed could be a winning trade, and the brachistochrone is the curve that gets the trade exactly right.

quiz.question: Two candidate curves connect A = (0, 0) to B = (L, H). Curve A is the straight line from A to B. Curve B drops vertically by H/4 in the first instant and then runs straight at a shallow angle to B. Without doing the full integral, which curve gets the bead to B faster, and why?
options: Curve B is faster. The bead reaches a substantial fraction of its final speed in the first quick drop and then covers most of its arc length at high v, while Curve A spends a long stretch near the top at slow speed where the integrand ds / √(2gy) is largest.
Curve A is faster, because the straight line is the shortest path between any two points, and the shortest path is always the fastest path.
The two curves take exactly the same time: energy conservation gives the bead the same final speed at B along any path between the same endpoints, so total descent time depends only on the endpoints.
Curve B is faster only if H is larger than L; for shallow descents the straight line wins.
quiz.explanation: Energy conservation pins down the bead's speed at each height (v = √(2gy)) but says nothing about how long the bead spends at each height. The straight line's average speed is roughly v(H/2) = √(gH), because the bead is moving slowly near the top and fast near the bottom and spends comparable time on each. Curve B is fast almost everywhere: it reaches v = √(gH/2) in the first short stretch and then sprints across most of its arc length close to √(2gH). Even though Curve B is slightly longer than Curve A, that small length penalty is far outweighed by the speed bonus. Option 2 conflates path length with path time, which would be correct only if v were constant along the curve. Option 3 reverses which quantity energy conservation controls: the final speed is shared by every path between the same endpoints, but the time is not. Option 4 invents a regime where the slow-near-top problem somehow disappears; it does not, and the straight line is beaten for every choice of L and H.

blocks.text: Johann Bernoulli's own solution turned the bead problem into an optics problem. The layered atmosphere had been the standard model for refraction since Ptolemy: a stack of thin horizontal slabs, each with its own refractive index n_i, light bending at every interface. A ray crossing from slab i to slab i+1 obeys Snell's law, n_i sin θ_i = n_{i+1} sin θ_{i+1}, where θ is the angle the ray makes with the vertical normal to the slabs. Rewrite n as c / v, where v is the speed of light in that slab, and Snell's law reads sin θ / v = const, the same value in every layer of the stack.

Now, said Bernoulli, picture the bead as if it were a ray of light passing through an infinitely fine stack of horizontal layers in which the speed depends only on the depth y. Energy conservation supplies the local speed for free: v(y) = √(2gy), the same formula as in section 2. Apply Snell's law for the layered medium to the bead. The angle θ becomes the angle the curve makes with the vertical at the current point, and the constant sin θ / v is now sin θ / √(2gy). Bernoulli's claim was that this Snell identity, written as a single equation that holds at every point along the bead's path, is the differential equation of the curve of fastest descent:

blocks.tex: \frac{\sin \theta}{\sqrt{2 g y}} \;=\; C \;\;\text{(constant along the brachistochrone)}
blocks.caption: θ is the angle between the curve's tangent and the vertical at the current point. y is the depth below the starting height A. g is gravity. C is a constant, the same at every point, fixed by which two endpoints A and B the curve must connect. The equation says: along the brachistochrone, the ratio of sin θ to the local speed √(2gy) takes one and the same value at every point, exactly the conserved ratio Snell's law forces on a light ray crossing a continuously layered medium.

blocks.text: The trick was outrageous as physics: a sliding bead is not light, and the analogy carries no first-principles justification. But the underlying mathematics was sound. Fermat had derived Snell's law from the principle of least time, which is the same kind of variational principle (an integral extremized over candidate paths) that Bernoulli was applying to the bead. Once you write sin θ as dx/ds with ds² = dx² + dy², the single Snell equation above unfolds into the differential equation y · (1 + (dy/dx)²) = 1 / (2 g C²), and that differential equation is the cycloid. Bernoulli published the cycloid in May 1697 with the Snell-from-optics argument as his complete derivation, without ever explaining why the analogy was allowed to work in the first place. Forty-eight years later, Euler would supply the explanation by writing down the calculus of variations from scratch.

blocks.caption: Three beads slide simultaneously from A at the top-left to B at the bottom-right under gravity, frictionless and starting from rest. The accent (rust) curve is the cycloid. The dotted gray line is the straight path A to B. The third curve is the test family y = H · (x/L)^p with exponent p set by the slider. Press release, watch the race. The cycloid arrives first for every p the slider can reach, and the slider reveals what kind of wrong each alternative is: shallow exponents (p < 1) drop the bead late and waste its slow stretch on the long run-in; steep exponents (p > 1) leave the bead slow for too long near the top and never quite catch up.

blocks.text: A cycloid is the curve traced by a fixed point on the rim of a circle that rolls without slipping along a straight line. The geometric construction predates the brachistochrone by a century. Mersenne studied cycloids in 1599, Galileo named them and tried (unsuccessfully) to compute the area under one arch by weighing paper cutouts, and Pascal devoted an entire 1659 treatise to them, posing a prize competition that drew solutions from Wallis, Wren, and Huygens. Take a circle of radius R rolling along the line y = 0 from left to right, with a marked point on the rim starting at the origin. When the circle has rotated through angle θ (measured in radians), the rolling-without-slipping condition puts the contact point on the line at x_contact = Rθ. The marked point is offset from the center by (-R sin θ, -R cos θ), and the center is at (Rθ, -R). Adding these gives the marked point's coordinates as a function of θ.

blocks.caption: θ is the angle through which the rolling circle has rotated since the marked point left the origin, in radians. R is the radius of the rolling circle, the one free parameter of the cycloid family. x(θ) is the horizontal coordinate of the marked point. y(θ) is its depth below the starting height. As θ runs from 0 to π the marked point sweeps the first half-arch, descending to its deepest position y = 2R at θ = π, where its tangent is horizontal.

blocks.text: Check that this curve satisfies the Snell-law identity from section 3. Differentiate the parametric form: dx/dθ = R(1 - cos θ) and dy/dθ = R sin θ, so the slope of the curve is dy/dx = sin θ / (1 - cos θ). Use the half-angle identities sin θ = 2 sin(θ/2) cos(θ/2) and 1 - cos θ = 2 sin²(θ/2) to write dy/dx = cos(θ/2) / sin(θ/2) = cot(θ/2). The angle the tangent makes with the vertical is then θ/2, because tan(angle from vertical) = dx/dy = 1 / cot(θ/2) = tan(θ/2). The sin of this angle is sin(θ/2), and the depth is y = 2R sin²(θ/2). Substituting:

blocks.caption: The left side is the Snell-law ratio sin(angle from vertical) divided by the local speed √(2gy), evaluated on the cycloid by substituting sin(angle) = sin(θ/2) and y = 2R sin²(θ/2). The sin(θ/2) in the numerator cancels the sin(θ/2) inside the square root in the denominator, leaving a constant 1/(2√(gR)) that does not depend on θ. So the cycloid satisfies Bernoulli's equation sin θ / √(2gy) = C with C = 1/(2√(gR)) at every point along its arch, term by term.

blocks.text: Christiaan Huygens, working in The Hague on a clock for the Dutch admiralty, had discovered another remarkable property of the cycloid in 1659, thirty-seven years before Bernoulli posed the brachistochrone problem at all. A bead released from rest on a cycloid reaches the lowest point in the same time no matter where on the arch it started. The cycloid is the tautochrone (Greek tautos for same, chronos for time), and Huygens built a pendulum clock whose bob swung along a cycloidal arc (enforced by cycloidal cheeks at the suspension) so the period would be independent of amplitude, the only mechanical clock of the seventeenth century whose timekeeping survived a heavy sea. So when Bernoulli's brachistochrone turned out to be a cycloid, it inherited not only the geometry of a rolling wheel but the timekeeping symmetry that had once been Huygens' livelihood.

quiz.question: Along the cycloid x(θ) = R(θ - sin θ), y(θ) = R(1 - cos θ), a bead released from rest at θ = 0 has speed v(θ) = √(2gR(1 - cos θ)) at angle θ. Compute the elapsed time t(θ) from the start of the descent, and use it to read off the total time of descent to the bottom (θ = π).
options: t(θ) = θ √(R/g): the elapsed time is exactly linear in θ. The total descent time from the cusp at θ = 0 to the bottom at θ = π is π √(R/g), and it depends only on the radius R, not on the horizontal extent of the descent.
t(θ) = √(2R(1 − cos θ)/g): elapsed time grows as the square root of depth, the same as for a bead in free fall.
t(θ) = R sin θ / √(gR): elapsed time grows like sin θ; the bead spends most of its time near θ = π/2 where the curve is steepest.
t(θ) cannot be written in closed form. Computing it requires numerical integration of T = ∫ ds / v along the cycloid.
quiz.explanation: The arc-length differential is ds = √((dx/dθ)² + (dy/dθ)²) dθ = √(R²(1 - cos θ)² + R² sin² θ) dθ = R √(2(1 - cos θ)) dθ, using sin² θ + (1 - cos θ)² = 2(1 - cos θ). The speed is v(θ) = √(2gR(1 - cos θ)). Divide: dt = ds / v = R √(2(1 - cos θ)) / √(2gR(1 - cos θ)) dθ = √(R/g) dθ. The (1 - cos θ) factor cancels, leaving an integrand that is independent of θ. Integrating gives t(θ) = θ √(R/g), so at the bottom (θ = π) the elapsed time is π √(R/g). This is the cleanest result in the lesson, and it is the same identity that makes the cycloid the tautochrone: equal angular intervals are covered in equal time, no matter where on the curve the bead started. Option 4 is the most tempting wrong answer because it generalizes from the difficulty of T for arbitrary curves to the cycloid; in fact the cycloid is the unique curve for which the integral collapses to a one-line formula, which is precisely what makes it the tautochrone. Options 2 and 3 get the units right but the θ-dependence wrong.

sections.heading: 5. The London packet, and what Euler did next

blocks.text: Newton's solution arrived in Bernoulli's mail with no signature. The geometric construction was immaculate and the diagram unmistakable: the cycloid, with a clean proof from first principles. Bernoulli's tanquam ex ungue leonem (I know the lion by his claw) entered scientific folklore and has been polished by retelling. Newton, semi-retired at the Mint, solving overnight what had taken the Bernoullis months. The story gets two things right and one thing wrong. Newton did solve it in one evening, on January 29th, 1697, after coming home from the Mint at four in the afternoon. He was indeed exhausted from running the Mint, where the great recoinage of English silver had absorbed his last eighteen months. The wrong part is the framing of the solution as casual genius. Newton's Principia (1687) had already contained the differential apparatus needed to attack the brachistochrone, and the calculus he had invented in the 1660s was the right tool for the job. He had the tools at hand and applied them carefully, not casually.

What the brachistochrone story actually changed was the kind of question physicists could ask. Up to 1696, calculus had been the technique for finding maxima and minima of functions of finitely many variables: at what x is f(x) smallest, at what (x, y) is g(x, y) smallest. The brachistochrone asks for the function (not the value) that minimizes an integral, a problem with infinitely many degrees of freedom (one per point along the candidate curve). No general technique existed. Each of the five 1697 solutions was an ad hoc construction tied to the particular form of the time integral T = ∫ ds / √(2gy). Bernoulli's Snell-law trick worked for that integrand and would not have worked for any other. Newton's geometric construction worked, too, by reading the curve off a clever auxiliary diagram, but it was equally tied to the special structure of the problem.

Leonhard Euler, in his 1744 Methodus inveniendi lineas curvas (Method of finding curves), set out to do better. He started with the question Bernoulli had answered for one specific integrand and asked the general version: what is the curve y(x) between fixed endpoints that extremizes the integral J[y] = ∫ F(x, y, y') dx, for an arbitrary integrand F? The answer was the calculus of variations. The necessary condition Euler derived is the same equation that, generalized by Lagrange forty years later, became the foundation of analytical mechanics:

blocks.caption: The Euler equation, the necessary condition for a curve y(x) to extremize the integral J[y] = ∫ F(x, y, y') dx between fixed endpoints. F is the integrand, a given function of x, y, and the slope y' = dy/dx. ∂F/∂y is the partial derivative with respect to y holding x and y' fixed. ∂F/∂y' is the partial derivative with respect to y' holding x and y fixed. d/dx is the total derivative along the curve. The equation reads: the variation of F with respect to y, balanced against the rate of change of its slope-wise variation along the curve, must vanish at every point of the extremizing curve.

blocks.text: Plug Bernoulli's brachistochrone integrand F = √((1 + y'²) / y) into the Euler equation and out drops the cycloid, with no analogy to optics required. The ad hoc trick of 1696 had become a special case of a general theorem. Forty more years and Joseph-Louis Lagrange replaced y(x) with a generalized coordinate q(t) and F with the Lagrangian T − V; the same equation became the equation of motion of every mechanical system, the result you met in the Lagrangian-mechanics lesson three days ago. The brachistochrone is where the chain begins. From a bead sliding down a wire in 1696 came the variational machinery that would later define Hamilton's principle for mechanics, the Einstein-Hilbert action for general relativity, and Feynman's path integral for quantum field theory. Johann Bernoulli, picking a problem to which he already knew the answer, opened a door whose far side was the entire structure of modern theoretical physics.

finalQuiz.question: A learner reads the lesson and concludes that the brachistochrone story is important because it identifies a particular pretty curve, the cycloid, as the answer to a particular optimization. Which statement most precisely captures what the brachistochrone story actually changed about how physics asks questions?
options: It was the first concrete problem to ask for the function (not the value) that extremizes an integral, with infinitely many degrees of freedom rather than finitely many. The 1697 solutions were ad hoc constructions tied to one specific integrand T = ∫ ds / √(2gy); Euler's 1744 Methodus inveniendi turned the ad hoc methods into a general theory, the calculus of variations, whose Euler-Lagrange equation became the language of every later variational principle (least action, Einstein-Hilbert, the path integral).
It proved that light and matter obey the same physical laws, because Bernoulli used Snell's law (an optics principle) to solve a mechanics problem.
It established that Newton was the greater mathematician of the seventeenth century, since he solved overnight what had taken the Bernoullis several months.
It established the cycloid as the most important curve in physics: nearly every later result in mechanics, optics, or quantum mechanics reduces in some way to the cycloid.
finalQuiz.explanation: The cycloid identification was the puzzle. The deeper consequence was the new kind of question itself. Instead of asking for the value of x that minimizes a finitely many-dimensional f(x), the brachistochrone asked for the function y(x) that minimizes the integral T[y] = ∫ ds / √(2gy). That is infinite-dimensional, and the methods of 1696 were specific tricks tied to the specific integrand. Euler's general theory replaced the tricks with a recipe, and Lagrange then used the recipe to rewrite all of mechanics. Option 2 misreads Bernoulli's Snell-law analogy as a physical identity; it was a mathematical device, and Bernoulli himself made no claim of a deeper unity of light and matter from it. Option 3 confuses speed of solution with depth of contribution; Newton was indeed fast, but the Bernoullis and Leibniz had been thinking about the broader class of variational problems for years and contributed more to the long arc of the subject. Option 4 inflates the role of the cycloid itself; the cycloid is a beautiful curve and appears in the brachistochrone, tautochrone, and a handful of related classical problems, but the general theory of variations did not single it out, and its importance does not extend much past these classical cases.
